title: Oxidation and reduction — the gain and loss of electrons
examTip: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). The oxidising agent GAINS electrons (and is itself reduced). The reducing agent LOSES electrons (and is itself oxidised). These feel backwards at first — learn them as a pair.
introTips: OIL RIG is the mnemonic you'll use for the rest of your chemistry career — make sure you know it cold.
In electrolysis: cathode = reduction (positive ions gain electrons). Anode = oxidation (negative ions lose electrons).
For oxidation states, the rules are fixed — practise using them on H₂SO₄ and KMnO₄ as examples.
intro: Every time you recharge your phone, rust forms on iron, or a bonfire burns, the same fundamental process is happening: electrons are moving between atoms. This transfer of electrons — called redox — is one of the most important processes in chemistry. It drives batteries, powers the cells in your body, and underpins the entire metals industry. Understanding redox means you can explain how anything from a fuel cell to a displacement reaction actually works at the atomic level.

keyPoints.heading: Oxidation and reduction — the definitions you need
keyPoints.content:
There are three ways to define oxidation and reduction, each useful in different situations:

In terms of oxygen: Oxidation = gain of oxygen. Reduction = loss of oxygen.
In terms of hydrogen: Oxidation = loss of hydrogen. Reduction = gain of hydrogen.
In terms of electrons (most important): Oxidation = loss of electrons. Reduction = gain of electrons.

Remember: OIL RIG — Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).

Redox reactions — reactions where both oxidation AND reduction happen simultaneously. You can never have one without the other. Example: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Magnesium is oxidised (gains oxygen / loses electrons: Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻). Oxygen is reduced (gains electrons: O₂ + 4e⁻ → 2O²⁻). Both happen together — it's redox.
writeBullets: Oxidation — gain of oxygen, loss of hydrogen, or loss of electrons
Reduction — loss of oxygen, gain of hydrogen, or gain of electrons
OIL RIG — Oxidation Is Loss (electrons), Reduction Is Gain (electrons)
Redox reaction — oxidation and reduction always happen simultaneously
2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO: Mg oxidised (loses electrons), O₂ reduced (gains electrons)

keyTerms.def: Gain of oxygen, loss of hydrogen, or loss of electrons
Loss of oxygen, gain of hydrogen, or gain of electrons
Mnemonic: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)
A reaction where both oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously

keyPoints.memTip: OIL RIG: write it on your hand if you have to. Oxidation Is Loss — the thing being oxidised LOSES electrons.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often mix up which substance is oxidised and which is reduced. The key is: track the electrons — where do they come from, where do they go?

keyPoints.heading: Oxidising agents and reducing agents
keyPoints.content:
An oxidising agent causes another substance to be oxidised — it does this by accepting electrons from it. In doing so, the oxidising agent is itself reduced (gains the electrons). An reducing agent causes another substance to be reduced — it does this by donating electrons. In doing so, the reducing agent is itself oxidised (loses electrons).

The trick: the oxidising agent GAINS electrons (is reduced). The reducing agent LOSES electrons (is oxidised). This feels counterintuitive — practise it.

Common oxidising agents: O₂, Cl₂, H₂O₂, concentrated H₂SO₄ — these readily accept electrons.
Common reducing agents: H₂, carbon (C), CO — these readily donate electrons and are used to extract metals.

Example — extraction of iron: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. CO is the reducing agent (loses oxygen / donates electrons → oxidised to CO₂). Fe₂O₃ is the oxidising agent (gains electrons → Fe is reduced).
writeBullets: Oxidising agent — causes oxidation; itself gains electrons (is reduced)
Reducing agent — causes reduction; itself loses electrons (is oxidised)
The oxidising agent is always the one that gets reduced in the reaction
Common oxidising agents: O₂, Cl₂, H₂O₂
Common reducing agents: H₂, C (carbon), CO — used in metal extraction
In Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂: CO is reducing agent, Fe₂O₃ is oxidising agent

keyTerms.def: A substance that causes another to be oxidised — it gains electrons and is itself reduced
A substance that causes another to be reduced — it loses electrons and is itself oxidised

keyPoints.memTip: The oxidising agent gets reduced (it 'takes' the electrons from the other substance). The reducing agent gets oxidised (it 'gives' electrons away).
keyPoints.tutor_note: A good exam question to practise: 'Identify the oxidising and reducing agents in the following reactions.' Students must state which is oxidised and which is reduced.

keyPoints.heading: Oxidation states (oxidation numbers)
keyPoints.content:
Oxidation state (oxidation number) is a formal number assigned to each atom in a compound showing its degree of oxidation. It lets you track exactly which atoms are oxidised and which are reduced in any reaction.

Rules for assigning oxidation states:
• An element in its standard state = 0 (e.g., Fe, O₂, Na, H₂)
• A monoatomic ion = its ionic charge (Na⁺ = +1, Cl⁻ = −1, Fe³⁺ = +3)
• Oxygen is almost always −2 (except peroxides: −1)
• Hydrogen is almost always +1 (except metal hydrides: −1)
• Sum of oxidation states in a neutral compound = 0
• Sum of oxidation states in a polyatomic ion = charge of the ion

Worked example — H₂SO₄: H = +1, O = −2. So: 2(+1) + S + 4(−2) = 0 → +2 + S − 8 = 0 → S = +6.

An increase in oxidation state = oxidation. A decrease = reduction.
writeBullets: Oxidation state — a number showing the degree of oxidation of an atom in a compound
Element in standard state = 0; monoatomic ion = its charge
Oxygen = −2 (usually); hydrogen = +1 (usually)
Neutral compound: oxidation states sum to zero
Increase in oxidation state = oxidised; decrease = reduced

keyTerms.def: A number assigned to an atom in a compound indicating its degree of oxidation; increases when oxidised, decreases when reduced
The Roman numeral in a compound name (e.g., iron(III)) gives the oxidation state of that element

keyPoints.memTip: Going up in oxidation state = oxidised. Going down = reduced. Think: up = losing electrons = oxidation.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Oxidation states are more commonly tested at higher tier. Practise finding the oxidation state of the 'unusual' element in compounds like KMnO₄ and Cr₂O₇²⁻.

keyPoints.heading: Redox in displacement reactions and electrolysis
keyPoints.content:
Displacement reactions are redox reactions. When a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive one from a solution, electrons transfer from the reactive metal to the metal ions in solution.

Example: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu
Half equations: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (zinc oxidised — loses electrons — reducing agent).
Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper reduced — gains electrons — oxidising agent).

Electrolysis is also redox:
At the cathode (negative electrode): positive ions gain electrons → reduction. Example: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu.
At the anode (positive electrode): negative ions lose electrons → oxidation. Example: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻.

Mnemonic: CROA — Cathode Reduction, Oxidation Anode.
writeBullets: Displacement reactions: more reactive metal loses electrons → oxidised; less reactive ion gains electrons → reduced
Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu: Zn is reducing agent (oxidised), Cu²⁺ is oxidising agent (reduced)
Electrolysis — cathode: reduction (positive ions gain electrons)
Electrolysis — anode: oxidation (negative ions lose electrons)
CROA: Cathode Reduction, Oxidation Anode
Half equations show electron transfer: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation) and Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (reduction)

keyTerms.def: A reaction where a more reactive metal takes the place of a less reactive metal in a compound; always a redox reaction
An equation showing only one half of a redox reaction — either the oxidation or the reduction step
The negative electrode in electrolysis; reduction occurs here (positive ions gain electrons)
The positive electrode in electrolysis; oxidation occurs here (negative ions lose electrons)

keyPoints.memTip: CROA: Cathode = Reduction, Oxidation = Anode. Or: 'CROC grows' — Cathode Reduction, Oxidation Cathode... wait, use CROA!
keyPoints.tutor_note: Displacement reactions often appear in the reactivity series topic — link redox terminology explicitly to those questions.

tables.title: Redox reaction examples — identifying oxidised/reduced species

0.question: In the reaction Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu, which species is oxidised?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Zinc loses electrons (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻) — it is oxidised. OIL: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons.
0.wrongFeedback: Zinc is oxidised — it loses electrons: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻. OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons.

1.question: What is the oxidation state of sulfur in H₂SO₄? (H = +1, O = −2)

2.question: In electrolysis, what happens at the cathode?
2.correctFeedback: Spot on! Cathode = reduction. Positive ions gain electrons at the cathode. CROA: Cathode Reduction, Oxidation Anode.
2.wrongFeedback: Cathode = reduction. Positive ions move to the cathode and GAIN electrons (are reduced). Remember CROA: Cathode Reduction, Oxidation Anode.

commonMistakes: Confusing oxidising agent and reducing agent — the oxidising agent is the one that gets REDUCED (it gains electrons from the other substance).
Saying 'the substance that gains oxygen is reduced' — gaining oxygen means being OXIDISED. Loss of oxygen = reduction.
Forgetting that redox always happens in pairs — you cannot have oxidation without reduction occurring simultaneously.
Getting oxidation states wrong by forgetting the rule that neutral compounds must sum to zero — always write out the algebra.
In electrolysis, confusing cathode and anode — cathode is negative and where reduction occurs; anode is positive and where oxidation occurs.
examTips: Always write OIL RIG at the top of any redox question as a quick reference — then identify which substance loses electrons (oxidised) and which gains (reduced).
When identifying oxidising/reducing agents, first identify what's oxidised and what's reduced — the oxidising agent is whatever caused the oxidation, which means it was reduced.
For oxidation state calculations, write the equation: known values + unknown = 0 (for neutral compounds). Solve algebraically.
In electrolysis questions, state both the electrode (cathode/anode), the process (reduction/oxidation), and write the half equation for full marks.
revisionCardBullets: Oxidation: gain of oxygen, loss of hydrogen, or loss of electrons
Reduction: loss of oxygen, gain of hydrogen, or gain of electrons
OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)
Redox: oxidation and reduction always happen together
Oxidising agent: gains electrons, is itself reduced
Reducing agent: loses electrons, is itself oxidised
Common oxidising agents: O₂, Cl₂, H₂O₂
Common reducing agents: H₂, C (carbon), CO
Oxidation state: 0 for elements; −2 for O; +1 for H (usually)
Neutral compound: oxidation states sum to zero
Increase in oxidation state = oxidised; decrease = reduced
